Episode 1 | [Daniel Percival](/wiki/Daniel_Percival_(director) "Daniel Percival (director)") | [Frank Spotnitz](/wiki/Frank_Spotnitz "Frank Spotnitz") | 12 August 2011 (2011-08-12) | 0.567[[4]](#cite_note-ep201rating-4) | 21 August 2011 (2011-08-21) | 1.137

infobox.key: John Porter (Armitage) is kidnapped by [Pakistani](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") terrorist Latif, who is masterminding "Project Dawn". As Porter is one of only two westerners who can identify Latif, Section 20 head Colonel Eleanor Grant (Mealing) appoints Sergeant Michael Stonebridge (Winchester) to find the other; dishonourably discharged Delta Force operative Damian Scott (Stapleton), in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). When Scott is brought to [London](/wiki/London "London"), Section 20 witness Porter's live execution. As the team mourn their loss, Scott realises that Porter used code in the execution video, directing the team to a hotel in [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), [India](/wiki/India "India"). Scott and Stonebridge's arrival is soon followed by a gang of terrorists (likely recruited by Latif from the insurgent group Lashkar-e-Taiba, which has conducted a number of similar terrorist attacks in India in real life), who hold everybody hostage and demand that someone named Mahmood reveals himself. Stonebridge and two of the hostages escape and meet with Scott; however, they find themselves surrounded when one of the hostages turns out to be the terrorist leader.

infobox.value: Episode 2 | Daniel Percival | Frank Spotnitz | 19 August 2011 (2011-08-19) | 0.169[[5]](#cite_note-5) | 28 August 2011 (2011-08-28) | 1.185

infobox.key: Scott and Stonebridge evade the terrorists and find Mahmood, who turns out to be a woman, Iman Zubedah ([Sasha Behar](/wiki/Sasha_Behar "Sasha Behar")). Zubedah reveals she knows the location of hidden [weapons of mass destruction](/wiki/Weapons_of_mass_destruction "Weapons of mass destruction") (WMD) that were to be planted in [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") to vindicate the [war](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"). After Scott is recaptured and taken to the lobby with the other hostages, Zubedah is escorted out by the terrorist leader, assumed to be Latif (Scott earlier admits that he never actually saw him). Stonebridge rescues Zubedah and kills the leader. When the Indian military storm the hotel, Scott fails to stop the terrorists from dropping a bomb, but Stonebridge arrives in time to catch it. Zubedah is later brought to Pakistani intelligence officer Jamal Ashkani (Mistry), who is revealed to be Latif; Zubedah is later found dead. After Scott is assigned to Section 20, he reveals to Stonebridge that Porter's code was directed at Scott; Porter no longer trusted Section 20, believing it is embroiled in a conspiracy involving Latif, the WMDs, his capture, and Scott's dishonourable discharge. Scott and Stonebdrige decide to investigate the possible corruption.

infobox.value: Episode 3 | Bill Eagles | Frank Spotnitz | 26 August 2011 (2011-08-26) | 0.255[[6]](#cite_note-6) | 4 September 2011 (2011-09-04) | 1.098

infobox.key: Section 20 set up base in Cape Town, South Africa to track former [Provisional Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army") (IRA) terrorist Daniel Connelly ([Liam Cunningham](/wiki/Liam_Cunningham "Liam Cunningham")), who is believed to be associated with Latif. Connelly and his gang rob an armoured truck, and [clone](/wiki/Cloning_(programming) "Cloning (programming)") a hard drive belonging to Kenneth Bratton's ([Alistair Petrie](/wiki/Alistair_Petrie "Alistair Petrie")) weapons company ATAT, which contains information on chemical weapons. Porter [previously](/wiki/Strike_Back_(TV_series)#ep1 "Strike Back (TV series)") rescued Bratton from Iraq in 2003. Connelly also has Bratton's family watched in order to get him to cooperate. The gang hires Buckley, an American hacker, to break into the hard drive, due to the fact that their previous hacker had been killed by one of the guards during the armored truck robbery. However, as Scott and Stonebridge chase him, Stonebridge is forced to kill him, forcing Scott to pose as Buckley and lead Section 20 to Connelly. When Scott meets the gang however, they capture him and send him to a farm where they strap a bomb to him. Stonebridge soon arrives at the farm, but as he is about to move in, the bomb detonates.

infobox.value: Episode 4 | Bill Eagles | Frank Spotnitz | 9 September 2011 (2011-09-09) | 0.212[[7]](#cite_note-7) | 11 September 2011 (2011-09-11) | 1.090

infobox.key: It is revealed that Scott is alive; Connelly directed the bomb away from his body as a test to see if he would call for help. When he passes by not doing so, Scott is told he is to override the security system of Bratton's secure facility, with the help of the hard drive and Major Oliver Sinclair ([Rhashan Stone](/wiki/Rhashan_Stone "Rhashan Stone")). Bratton calls Captain Kate Marshall (Birthistle) for help in his family's protection. Kate learns that ATAT was under pressure to plant [VX nerve gas](/wiki/VX_(nerve_agent) "VX (nerve agent)") in Iraq. During the meeting, the two are kidnapped by Connolly's men. Stonebridge tracks her to the facility Scott was sent to hack into. Connelly gains access to a package of VX before killing Bratton. Though Scott and Stonebridge capture Connelly and confiscate the VX, they find Kate rigged with a bomb. Connelly promises to disarm it if they let him go, however he goes back on the deal and escapes, while Kate dies in the ensuing explosion. Grant later follows Connelly to [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique") to execute him.

***Strike Back: Project Dawn***, as it is known in the United Kingdom, is a ten-part British action television serial and is the second series of *[Strike Back](/wiki/Strike_Back_(TV_series) "Strike Back (TV series)")*. However, the only cast member to return from the first series was [Richard Armitage](/wiki/Richard_Armitage_(actor) "Richard Armitage (actor)") in the first episode. The main cast for the series includes [Philip Winchester](/wiki/Philip_Winchester "Philip Winchester"), [Sullivan Stapleton](/wiki/Sullivan_Stapleton "Sullivan Stapleton"), [Amanda Mealing](/wiki/Amanda_Mealing "Amanda Mealing"), [Eva Birthistle](/wiki/Eva_Birthistle "Eva Birthistle"), [Michelle Lukes](/wiki/Michelle_Lukes "Michelle Lukes") and [Rhashan Stone](/wiki/Rhashan_Stone "Rhashan Stone"). The series follows the actions of Section 20, a secret branch of the British [Secret Intelligence Service](/wiki/Secret_Intelligence_Service "Secret Intelligence Service") (MI6), particularly two of its officers, Michael Stonebridge (Winchester) and former American Delta Force operative Damian Scott (Stapleton). Section 20 leads a worldwide manhunt to find and capture Pakistani terrorist Latif, who is plotting an operation of his known as "Project Dawn".

[Sky](/wiki/BSkyB "BSkyB") announced *Strike Back* would be returning for a third series after the first aired. They entered a co-production deal with the American [premium cable](/wiki/Pay_television "Pay television") network [Cinemax](/wiki/Cinemax "Cinemax"), who wanted to introduce new original drama series to the network. In order to prepare for their roles several main cast members spent a month training with former officers of the [Special Air Service](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service") and [Special Boat Service](/wiki/Special_Boat_Service "Special Boat Service"). Filming began in February 2011 and took place across [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary").

The series began broadcasting on Cinemax in the United States on 12 August 2011, with the premiere being seen by nearly 1.1 million viewers, the highest ratings achieved by the network since 2005. It later began broadcasting on [Sky1](/wiki/Sky1 "Sky1") in the United Kingdom on 21 August with higher ratings than that of the first series. Critical reaction of the series was generally favourable in the United States, however British reviewers were more mixed. Reviewers made comparisons to various other television dramas including *[24](/wiki/24_(TV_series) "24 (TV series)")*, *[Sleeper Cell](/wiki/Sleeper_Cell_(TV_series) "Sleeper Cell (TV series)")*, *[Spooks](/wiki/Spooks_(TV_series) "Spooks (TV series)")* and *[The Unit](/wiki/The_Unit "The Unit")*. *Project Dawn* was released on [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") in [Region 2](/wiki/DVD_region_code#2 "DVD region code") on 14 November 2011. In October 2011, Cinemax announced it has renewed *Strike Back* for another 10-part series,[[1]](#cite_note-sb3-1) to be titled *[Strike Back: Vengeance](/wiki/Strike_Back:_Vengeance "Strike Back: Vengeance")*.[[2]](#cite_note-digspyveng-2)

## Cast and characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cast and characters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/PhilipWinchesterApr10.jpg/250px-PhilipWinchesterApr10.jpg)](/wiki/File:PhilipWinchesterApr10.jpg)

[Philip Winchester](/wiki/Philip_Winchester "Philip Winchester") portrays Michael Stonebridge.

[Philip Winchester](/wiki/Philip_Winchester "Philip Winchester") plays Sergeant Michael Stonebridge, a [Royal Marine](/wiki/Royal_Marine "Royal Marine") commando and former [Special Boat Service](/wiki/Special_Boat_Service "Special Boat Service") (SBS) operator,[[13]](#cite_note-13) now assigned to Section 20 who is partnered with Scott to help track down a kidnapped Porter.[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14) Stonebridge works for Section 20 and is in charge of his team on the ground. Although the character is married to his wife Kerry ([Alexandra Moen](/wiki/Alexandra_Moen "Alexandra Moen")), he has an affair with colleague Kate Marshall (played by Birthistle). Winchester describes Stonebridge as "dedicated, disciplined, always plays by the rules and often saves the day. Stonebridge directs his energy trying to protecting others," though as the story unfolds, the audience would see that Stonebridge "is not the knight in shining armour."[[15]](#cite_note-skyintvwinchester-15)

[Sullivan Stapleton](/wiki/Sullivan_Stapleton "Sullivan Stapleton") plays Damien Scott, a former United States [Delta Force](/wiki/Delta_Force "Delta Force") operator who is familiar with the antagonists of the second series, and an acquaintance of John Porter. He is recruited by Section 20 to help Sergeant Michael Stonebridge find and rescue the kidnapped Porter.[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14) Stapleton describes Scott as cocky, rebellious and arrogant, and that it was good to have somebody like him in Section 20 because "he is the one that usually goes in first and takes the bullets, whereas Stonebridge arrives at the last minute and saves the day."[[16]](#cite_note-skyintvstapleton-16) The actor opined that Scott is chosen for the dangerous role because he is daring and does not have any immediate family members who rely on him. Of Scott and Stonebridge's working relationship, Stapleton said "they have a professional respect for each other" but start to annoy each other. When the actor first read the script for *Project Dawn*, he saw himself for the role as Scott.[[16]](#cite_note-skyintvstapleton-16) Because Stapleton is an Australian actor, he had to speak with an American accent throughout the series. He was wary whether he did it right because he barely worked with Americans on the show, but believed he did okay when HBO, the network that operated Cinemax, was satisfied with it.[[17]](#cite_note-metrointv-17) Stapleton has signed up for another *Strike Back* series should it be commissioned.[[18]](#cite_note-heraldsun-18)

[Amanda Mealing](/wiki/Amanda_Mealing "Amanda Mealing") plays Colonel Eleanor Grant, a "smart, tough military leader who is skilled with a gun and diplomacy, but knows when to break a rule or two."[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14) She is the new head of Section 20, replacing Hugh Collinson from the first series. Mealing described Grant as "hard as nails," "very contained, very direct," and "very driven."[[19]](#cite_note-skyintvmealing-19) Grant is also very distant from her team; "she commands so she is not their pal but, at the same time, the guys are like her family. They spend so much time together moving from place to place. But, in a kind of motherly way, she has to keep them in check and sometimes they need a firm hand."[[19]](#cite_note-skyintvmealing-19) The actress called her role "the best role I've ever had," adding "when I'm doing action scenes like flying in helicopters, the hardest thing is to keep a serious face as all I want to do is scream for joy."[[20]](#cite_note-tvsatweek-20) She researched for her role by taking advice from a female officer in a similar high-ranking position as Grant is. One piece of advice the officer gave Mealing was "you mustn't try to be one of the boys or they'll lose respect for you, but you've got to be in charge."[[20]](#cite_note-tvsatweek-20)

[Eva Birthistle](/wiki/Eva_Birthistle "Eva Birthistle") plays Captain Kate Marshall, a "crack commando" who oversees Section 20's military personnel.[[20]](#cite_note-tvsatweek-20) She and Stonebridge have an affair. The actress was taken by the script, and although she was cast for the role, she had not done action in her past acting career, and felt it would be a "fun experience" to play the character. When Birthistle went through military training, her goal was "just to keep up with everybody and not give up, pass out, throw up or cry."[[21]](#cite_note-skyintvbirthistle-21) [Rhashan Stone](/wiki/Rhashan_Stone "Rhashan Stone") plays Major Oliver Sinclair,[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14) Grant's right-hand man who helps her gather intelligence. Mealing called their working relationship as "very strong" and "simpatico."[[19]](#cite_note-skyintvmealing-19) Sinclair is the section's second-in-command.[[22]](#cite_note-makingof-22) [Michelle Lukes](/wiki/Michelle_Lukes "Michelle Lukes") plays Sergeant Julia Richmond,[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14) one of the newer recruits of Section 20. Lukes describes her character as "exceptionally bright and technically brilliant," with the ability to speak seven languages.[[22]](#cite_note-makingof-22) [Jimi Mistry](/wiki/Jimi_Mistry "Jimi Mistry") plays the antagonist Latif, the terrorist mastermind behind Project Dawn.[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14)[[22]](#cite_note-makingof-22) [Richard Armitage](/wiki/Richard_Armitage_(actor) "Richard Armitage (actor)") returns as John Porter, the protagonist of the original *Strike Back* series, in the first episode. Originally, Armitage's role in the series was not clarified when production was announced. In January 2011, the *[Herald Sun](/wiki/Herald_Sun "Herald Sun")* reported he dropped out of the series because he would be playing [Thorin Oakenshield](/wiki/Thorin_Oakenshield "Thorin Oakenshield") in [*The Hobbit* films](/wiki/The_Hobbit_(film_series) "The Hobbit (film series)").[[18]](#cite_note-heraldsun-18) However, it was later reported he would return for the second series in a secondary rather than primary role.[[23]](#cite_note-sky2ndseries-23)

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/FrankSpotnitz.jpg/250px-FrankSpotnitz.jpg)](/wiki/File:FrankSpotnitz.jpg)

[Frank Spotnitz](/wiki/Frank_Spotnitz "Frank Spotnitz") wrote the first four episodes of *Strike Back: Project Dawn*.

*Strike Back* began as a six-part series from Sky1, based on a book of the same name from former [Special Air Service](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service") (SAS) soldier turned novelist [Chris Ryan](/wiki/Chris_Ryan "Chris Ryan"). It transmitted two episodes weekly from 5 to 19 May 2010.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The first two episodes more than tripled Sky1's audience share in its timeslot in the past three months.[[25]](#cite_note-guardianpremrate-25) In August 2010, Sky1 controller [Stuart Murphy](/wiki/Stuart_Murphy "Stuart Murphy") commissioned a second series to consist of a longer run of ten episodes. [Left Bank Pictures](/wiki/Left_Bank_Pictures "Left Bank Pictures") continued their role as the production company.[[26]](#cite_note-guardian-26) In February 2011, it was announced that Sky entered a co-production deal with the American television network [Cinemax](/wiki/Cinemax "Cinemax"), as the network wanted to introduce new drama series. *Strike Back* became one of two television projects to be produced by British and American networks in 2011, the other being *[Torchwood: Miracle Day](/wiki/Torchwood:_Miracle_Day "Torchwood: Miracle Day")*, which was produced between the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") and [Starz](/wiki/Starz_(TV_channel) "Starz (TV channel)").[[27]](#cite_note-ew-27)

[Frank Spotnitz](/wiki/Frank_Spotnitz "Frank Spotnitz"), Richard Zajdlic, [Tony Saint](/wiki/Tony_Saint "Tony Saint") and Simon Burke were contracted as the series writers, while Alex Holmes, Bill Eagles, [Paul Wilmshurst](/wiki/Paul_Wilmshurst "Paul Wilmshurst") and [Daniel Percival](/wiki/Daniel_Percival_(director) "Daniel Percival (director)") serve as the series directors. [Andy Harries](/wiki/Andy_Harries "Andy Harries") and Elaine Pyke were the show's executive producers, with Percival and Spotnitz working as Co-Executive Producers. In addition, Michael Casey, Trevor Hopkins and Sue De Beauvoir were the series producers, with Bill Shepard serving as co-producer.[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14) Spotnitz wrote the first episodes. He was approached to write for the series because the producers needed somebody to reimagine the show as Armitage was committed to *The Hobbit* in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), by introducing two new leads. Spotnitz described the process; "We had this great format, this great world of 'Strike Back' and then we had to imagine who were the new characters who were going to drive the show in the second year."[[28]](#cite_note-28) Before *Project Dawn* was even announced, the crew were scouting for locations in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), where the first series was shot, as well as investigating other parts of the world including the United States, South America, and Europe.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Training

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Training")]

> "We went on an overnight military tactical mission. The men that trained us really put us through it. I slept in my kit because I knew they were going to wake us up in the middle of the night and make us run up the hill."

—[Sullivan Stapleton](/wiki/Sullivan_Stapleton "Sullivan Stapleton") on the training[[16]](#cite_note-skyintvstapleton-16)

Before filming began, several of the main cast members were sent to a boot camp in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and went through intensive and rigorous military training by former SAS and SBS soldiers. Stapleton and Winchester had more training than Birthistle, who just wanted to make sure she could at least be a believable soldier. The actors were trained in several aspects including running, teamwork, and weapons.[[21]](#cite_note-skyintvbirthistle-21) Mealing meanwhile, did not go through as much training as the other actors, but did spend mornings training with a commander for the SBS who is also a military adviser on the show.[[19]](#cite_note-skyintvmealing-19) Winchester recalled, "Sully and I would meet every morning at 6am. We would run to a chosen location and on the way we had to memorise street names and directions and then our trainers would say 'that car that you just passed what was the licence plate number?' Our trainers were ex-SAS guys who made us study everything in detail, for example, we learnt step by step how to enter a room in twos, then alone. At the same time, we had to be constantly aware of where our weapon was trained."[[15]](#cite_note-skyintvwinchester-15)

The physical trainers also had to ensure that not only do the actors look like soldiers before filming, but they had to remain that way throughout the shoot.[[15]](#cite_note-skyintvwinchester-15) In addition, Stapleton and Winchester had to lose and gain weight, respectively. Winchester had to be on a diet of 4,000 calories a day, which included taking [protein shakes](/wiki/Bodybuilding_supplement#Protein "Bodybuilding supplement") between meals.[[30]](#cite_note-winchsullyintv-30) The actor described the diet as "a workout in itself," having found it "exhausting" eating more food than he was used to. In total, training took approximately one month.[[15]](#cite_note-skyintvwinchester-15)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Filming")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Hout_Bay_and_surrounding_mountains.jpg/250px-Hout_Bay_and_surrounding_mountains.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hout_Bay_and_surrounding_mountains.jpg)

The series was largely filmed in [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"), South Africa, with some scenes being shot in the suburb of [Hout Bay](/wiki/Hout_Bay "Hout Bay") (pictured).

Filming began in February 2011,[[27]](#cite_note-ew-27) and concluded in the late summer (from the Northern Hemisphere's perspective).[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14) Filming largely took place in South Africa, particularly in [Cape Town](/wiki/Cape_Town "Cape Town"),[[30]](#cite_note-winchsullyintv-30) but also in [Durban](/wiki/Durban "Durban") and [Springbok, Northern Cape](/wiki/Springbok,_Northern_Cape "Springbok, Northern Cape"). Outside South Africa the series was also filmed in [Budapest](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest"), [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"),[[31]](#cite_note-31) and the UK.[[18]](#cite_note-heraldsun-18) Among the separate locations the series takes place in, the New Delhi hotel in the first two episodes were shot in Cape Town, and the suburbs of [Hout Bay](/wiki/Hout_Bay "Hout Bay") doubled as [Mozambique](/wiki/Mozambique "Mozambique").[[20]](#cite_note-tvsatweek-20) Throughout the shoot the actors were allowed to perform the majority of their own stunts. Military advisors were on hand to teach the actors how to perform certain stunts, and to tell them what they did wrong during the take.[[30]](#cite_note-winchsullyintv-30) The series includes a number of sex scenes, many of which were performed by Stapleton. In an interview with the British newspaper *[Metro](/wiki/Metro_(British_newspaper) "Metro (British newspaper)")*, the actor stated "I've done my fair share of nude scenes in the past but not as much as this – not as risqué, anyway," adding that while he got used to it, he was always "a bit nervous running around in front of 50 people with no clothes on. The sex scenes were usually with an actress I'd just met, so it's like: 'How's it going? I'm Sullivan and this is me nude.' It's nerve-racking at first but you have as much fun as you can with it."[[17]](#cite_note-metrointv-17)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Release")]

### Broadcast and ratings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Broadcast and ratings")]

*Project Dawn* began broadcasting on 12 August 2011 in the United States.[[14]](#cite_note-tvbynumb-14) The first telecast at 10 pm was seen by 567,000 viewers, while the 11 pm and midnight encores almost doubled viewership, adding 500,000, giving a total of nearly 1.1 million viewers.[[4]](#cite_note-ep201rating-4) Although Cinemax is available in 16.7 million homes at the time of the broadcast, it was the best ratings performance for the network since a broadcast of the film *[Titanic](/wiki/Titanic_(1997_film) "Titanic (1997 film)")* in 2005.[[32]](#cite_note-dyk-32) It was also on-par with the pilot of the Starz series *[Spartacus: Blood and Sand](/wiki/Spartacus:_Blood_and_Sand "Spartacus: Blood and Sand")* in 2010, which also aired on the same timeslot.[[32]](#cite_note-dyk-32) In the United Kingdom, the series began broadcast on 21 August 2011.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The first episode, airing at 9 pm was seen by a total of 616,000 viewers, with an audience share of 2.6 per cent.[[34]](#cite_note-premiererate-34) As far as overnight ratings are concerned, it performed better than the first episode of the previous series, which was seen by 398,000, and a 1.7 per cent audience share.[[25]](#cite_note-guardianpremrate-25)

### Home video release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Home video release")]

*Project Dawn* was released on both [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") and [Blu-ray Disc](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc"), published by [2entertain](/wiki/2entertain "2entertain"), in the United Kingdom on 14 November 2011.[[35]](#cite_note-35) It was released with an "18" [British Board of Film Classification](/wiki/British_Board_of_Film_Classification "British Board of Film Classification") (BBFC) certificate (indicating it is unsuitable for viewers under the age of 18 years). Special features include *Top Secrets: Making Strike Back: Project Dawn*, *Firearms Training* and *Scott Vs Igor Fight Scene* featurettes, as well as [broadcast titles](/wiki/Opening_credits "Opening credits").[[36]](#cite_note-statmass-36) In the United States the series was released on DVD and Blu-ray on 7 August 2012, with the inclusion of [audio commentaries](/wiki/Audio_commentary "Audio commentary") by Daniel Percival.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Critical reactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Critical reactions")]

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: United States")]

> "If something has been missing from your TV screen since "[24](/wiki/24_(TV_series) "24 (TV series)")" went off the air, like an unapologetic, fist-pumping, nonstop action thriller with compelling good guys and loathsome bad guys, Cinemax's new "Strike Back" needs to be your appointment television for the next 10 weeks. It would be plenty good enough if "Strike Back" were just a TV reincarnation of all those [Steven Seagal](/wiki/Steven_Seagal "Steven Seagal") action flicks from a few years back. Because, sadly, the industry seems to have given up on that kind of popcorn thriller, figuring the audience either prefers special effects or is playing video games."

—David Hinkley, *[New York Daily News](/wiki/Daily_News_(New_York) "Daily News (New York)")*[[38]](#cite_note-nynews-38)

The American review site [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic") rated the series a 64 out of 100, indicating "generally favorable" reviews from 12 critics.[[39]](#cite_note-39) David Hinkley of the *[New York Daily News](/wiki/Daily_News_(New_York) "Daily News (New York)")* rated it four stars out of five, stating it is "a little cartoonish in the same way [Seagal](/wiki/Steven_Seagal "Steven Seagal") or [Arnold Schwarzenegger](/wiki/Arnold_Schwarzenegger "Arnold Schwarzenegger") action movies were cartoonish, exaggerating characters and action. But "Strike Back" also differs sharply and significantly in that the villains here aren't the random abstract caricatures from those films, like throwback Nazis or Jamaican drug lords," adding that the villains give the series "an added level of intensity, because we have a more visceral sense of the potential consequences."[[38]](#cite_note-nynews-38) Linda Stasi of the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")* rated it three out of four stars, summing up the series as; "Good action, good characters and, besides, it's the only show in the history of TV where the term, "[fucking prick]" is code. I mean, you gotta love that, no?"[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Aaron Riccio of *[Slant Magazine](/wiki/Slant_(magazine) "Slant (magazine)")* also rated it three out of four stars, stated "*Strike Back* isn't brilliant television, but it's plenty entertaining, and by fitting the action of *24* with the grit of *[The Unit](/wiki/The_Unit "The Unit")* (and the nudity of Cinemax), it fills a .22 caliber hole in American television."[[41]](#cite_note-slate-41) However Riccio added that the downside to the series was that "not enough time is given over to the fractious "buddy-cop" relationship between the two main characters, Scott and Stonebridge, whereas quite a bit of time is spent with the villains."[[41]](#cite_note-slate-41) [Hank Stuever](/wiki/Hank_Stuever "Hank Stuever") of *[The Washington Post](/wiki/The_Washington_Post "The Washington Post")* called it "surprisingly stylish and addictive," adding "Fans of "*24*," which bowed last year, still ask me how to fill that void. They want just enough intelligence-agency hooey to make the unbelievable seem somewhat real. Until "*Strike Back,*" I didn't have a good answer."[[42]](#cite_note-washpost-42) Stuever lauded Scott and Stapleton's portrayal, stating he is "the real find here, a thoroughly unlikable loser" who "saves the show from a dour sense of duty," adding that Stapleton "seems to have been created in a laboratory experiment that grafted [Ewan McGregor](/wiki/Ewan_McGregor "Ewan McGregor")'s personality and smile onto [Hugh Jackman](/wiki/Hugh_Jackman "Hugh Jackman")'s body – with more than adequate results."[[42]](#cite_note-washpost-42)

Matt Fowler of [IGN](/wiki/IGN "IGN") rated it seven out of ten, noting that American viewers do not have to see the first series to be caught up, but added "British fans might lament the absence of Richard Armitage's John Porter who was the ass-kicking hero of the original series."[[43]](#cite_note-43) Mike Hale of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* described the series as a "variation on "*24*" that offers reasonably competent action scenes, depressingly casual depictions of torture and death, and a comic-book conspiracy story line while also being an efficient nudity delivery system."[[44]](#cite_note-44) Brent MacKnight rated the series five out of ten, calling it a "decent enough action yarn with slick production values," but was critical that the series was "more concerned with gratuitous nudity [...] each episode includes a lifetime's worth of breasts and butt cheeks–than creating a story with any substance, character, or emotional weight."[[45]](#cite_note-45) [Rob Owen](/wiki/Rob_Owen_(journalist) "Rob Owen (journalist)") of the *[Pittsburgh Post-Gazette](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Post-Gazette "Pittsburgh Post-Gazette")* was more critical of the series, stating "At first, "*Strike Back*" seems like a highbrow series on the order of "[*[Spooks](/wiki/Spooks_(TV_series) "Spooks (TV series)")*]," but after a few minutes it "conforms to the Skinemax nickname with a graphic sex scene," with "multiple exclamations of the f-word and bloodshed galore."[[46]](#cite_note-pittzgazette-46) Owen summed "*Strike Back* shows off high production values and an intense, fast-paced story reminiscent of *24* or *[Sleeper Cell](/wiki/Sleeper_Cell_(TV_series) "Sleeper Cell (TV series)")* that occasionally dips into the ridiculous [...] It's too bad the show's graphic nature, especially the bloodshed, is so off-putting as to make the series unwatchable."[[46]](#cite_note-pittzgazette-46)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Strike_Back:_Project_Dawn&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

After the series premiered in the United Kingdom, British critics were mixed in their reviews. Jonathan Bernstein of *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* gave the series a positive review, stating "It's awesome! Everything that's capable of exploding explodes. What was a decent but unexceptional military action show is now *Naked 24*."[[47]](#cite_note-47) Jonathan Angwin of CultBox rated it three out of five stars, called it "the body of *Spooks* with very little of the brains," adding that the series is "silly, immature, shocking, but undoubtedly entertaining and certainly not boring."[[48]](#cite_note-48) Patrick Samuel of Static Mass also rated *Project Dawn* three out of five stars, stating that the main story was "an intriguing one with implications of a conspiracy deep within MI6," but was critical that the series mostly focused on Scott rather than the other main characters, the movement of the storyline, and that Scott and Stonebridge "seemed to be most inapt pair to lead missions as they blow their cover on several occasions, endangering others."[[36]](#cite_note-statmass-36) However, Samuel praised the series' action sequences, saying that they "[did] not disappoint."[[36]](#cite_note-statmass-36)

Paul Dean of Faded Glamour believed that *Strike Back* "remains pretty good at what it does—providing Sky viewers with their weekly *[Die Hard](/wiki/Die_Hard "Die Hard")* fix," adding "series two won't win any new converts, but it looks very much like it will continue to serve up the same pacey, unpretentious and unambiguous action as its predecessor."[[49]](#cite_note-49) Jack Foley of IndieLondon rated it 2.5 out of five. Foley felt the death of Porter in the first episode was "striking" since Armitage was the "main reason for watching."[[50]](#cite_note-indielondon-50) The reviewer also believed that while the first series "offered a keen mix of *Spooks* and *24* that expertly blended top-drawer action with some emotional complexity and intrigue," the second series "already feels inferior to both by virtue of the fact that its brains appear to lie in its pants, or blown from Porter's head."[[50]](#cite_note-indielondon-50) Keith Watson of *[Metro](/wiki/Metro_(British_newspaper) "Metro (British newspaper)")* stated that *Project Dawn* was merely "gung-ho nonsense."[[51]](#cite_note-51)
